card.indications: Treatment of hyperthyroidism due to Graves disease or toxic multinodular goiter, preparation for thyroidectomy or radioactive iodine therapy, and control of thyrotoxicosis when other definitive treatment is delayed.
card.mechanism: Inhibits thyroid peroxidase, blocking oxidation of iodide and coupling of iodotyrosines, which reduces synthesis of thyroid hormones. It does not inactivate hormone already stored in the thyroid.
card.dosing: Dose depends on severity of hyperthyroidism and thyroid-function results. Adult initial dosing is commonly divided every 8 hours, with lower maintenance dosing once control is achieved. Use the lowest effective dose and adjust from free T4, T3, and TSH trends.
card.routeAdministration: Give at the same times each day, consistently with or without food. Verify baseline CBC and liver tests when ordered. Do not stop abruptly without prescriber direction.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to methimazole or product components. Avoid routine use during the first trimester of pregnancy when propylthiouracil is preferred because of congenital-malformation risk.
card.warnings: Can cause agranulocytosis, aplastic anemia, severe liver injury, vasculitis, and fetal harm. Fever or sore throat may be the first sign of dangerous neutropenia and requires immediate evaluation.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Rash, itching, nausea, vomiting, altered taste, headache, drowsiness, joint pain, and mild leukopenia.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Agranulocytosis, pancytopenia, aplastic anemia, severe hepatitis or cholestatic jaundice, vasculitis, hypoprothrombinemia, and congenital abnormalities with early-pregnancy exposure.
card.interactions: Anticoagulant response may change as thyroid status normalizes. Beta-blocker and digoxin requirements may change. Iodine-containing products can alter the response to antithyroid therapy.
card.monitoring: Monitor free T4, T3, TSH, clinical response, CBC for infection symptoms, liver tests for hepatic symptoms, skin reactions, and pregnancy status when relevant.
card.patientTeaching: Report fever, sore throat, mouth ulcers, unusual bruising, jaundice, dark urine, severe fatigue, or rash immediately. Keep laboratory appointments and discuss pregnancy plans promptly.
card.nutrition: Take consistently with or without food. Avoid starting iodine or kelp supplements without approval because they can interfere with thyroid control.

card.indications: Treatment of selected cancers, rheumatoid arthritis, severe psoriasis, and other immune-mediated disorders. Route and regimen vary widely by diagnosis.
card.mechanism: Inhibits folate-dependent enzymes, including dihydrofolate reductase, reducing DNA synthesis and cell replication. At lower weekly doses it also produces anti-inflammatory and immunomodulating effects.
card.dosing: Dosing is indication specific. For many noncancer conditions it is taken once weekly, never once daily. Cancer regimens may use higher or more frequent doses with leucovorin rescue and specialized monitoring. Renal impairment requires dose adjustment or avoidance.
card.routeAdministration: Verify indication, route, exact dose, and day of the week before every administration. Use hazardous-drug precautions. Ensure appropriate hydration and leucovorin rescue when ordered. Intrathecal preparations must be preservative free.
card.contraindications: Pregnancy for nonmalignant indications, breastfeeding, alcoholism or chronic liver disease, significant immunodeficiency, severe blood dyscrasias, and hypersensitivity. Additional contraindications depend on indication and formulation.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include embryo-fetal toxicity, severe hypersensitivity, serious or fatal bone-marrow, hepatic, pulmonary, renal, gastrointestinal, and skin toxicity, and fatal medication errors when weekly therapy is mistakenly taken daily.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, mouth sores, fatigue, diarrhea, decreased appetite, mild hair thinning, rash, and elevated liver enzymes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Myelosuppression, infection, hepatotoxicity or cirrhosis, pneumonitis, renal failure, severe mucositis, GI bleeding or perforation, severe skin reactions, tumor lysis syndrome, and fetal death.
card.interactions: NSAIDs, penicillins, sulfonamides, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, proton-pump inhibitors, nephrotoxic drugs, and other antifolate or myelosuppressive agents can increase toxicity. Live vaccines should generally be avoided.
card.monitoring: Monitor CBC, renal function, liver tests, pregnancy status, pulmonary symptoms, mouth and GI toxicity, infection, skin reactions, hydration, and methotrexate concentrations in high-dose regimens.
card.patientTeaching: Confirm the exact weekly day for noncancer treatment. Never take an extra or daily dose. Report fever, mouth sores, bruising, bleeding, shortness of breath, severe diarrhea, rash, or reduced urine. Use reliable contraception as directed.
card.nutrition: Avoid alcohol. Maintain hydration unless restricted. Take folic acid or leucovorin exactly as prescribed and avoid unapproved folate supplements during high-dose cancer therapy.

card.indications: Treatment of hypertension. It has a long history of use for chronic hypertension during pregnancy when clinically appropriate.
card.mechanism: Converted in the CNS to alpha-methylnorepinephrine, which stimulates central alpha2 receptors and reduces sympathetic outflow, lowering peripheral vascular resistance.
card.dosing: Adult oral therapy commonly begins at 250 mg two to three times daily and is titrated gradually. Usual maintenance dosing is divided two to four times daily. Reduce dosing in renal impairment and older adults.
card.routeAdministration: Check blood pressure and pulse before administration. Give consistently with or without food. Titrate gradually and monitor more closely during the first months of therapy.
card.contraindications: Active hepatic disease, prior methyldopa-associated liver disorder, concurrent monoamine oxidase inhibitor use, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause severe liver injury, positive direct Coombs test with hemolytic anemia, sedation, depression, orthostatic hypotension, bradycardia, and rare blood dyscrasias.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, fatigue, dizziness, dry mouth, headache, weakness, edema, and orthostatic hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Hepatitis, hepatic necrosis, hemolytic anemia, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, severe bradycardia, heart failure, and neuroleptic malignant syndrome after abrupt withdrawal in rare cases.
card.interactions: Other antihypertensives and alcohol increase hypotension or sedation. Iron supplements can reduce methyldopa absorption. Monoamine oxidase inhibitors are contraindicated.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, pulse, orthostatic symptoms, liver tests especially early in therapy, CBC and direct Coombs testing during prolonged treatment, edema, mood, and renal function.
card.patientTeaching: Rise slowly and avoid driving until sedation is known. Report fever, extreme fatigue, jaundice, dark urine, pale skin, shortness of breath, or unusual bruising. Do not stop suddenly without guidance.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Separate iron supplements from methyldopa as directed. Limit alcohol and follow prescribed sodium guidance.

card.indications: Treatment of inflammatory, allergic, autoimmune, pulmonary, neurologic, hematologic, and endocrine disorders, including acute exacerbations that require systemic corticosteroid therapy.
card.mechanism: Binds intracellular glucocorticoid receptors and alters gene transcription, suppressing inflammatory mediators and immune responses while affecting carbohydrate, protein, fat, and bone metabolism.
card.dosing: Dose is highly indication and severity specific. Short oral tapers, daily therapy, pulse IV dosing, and intra-articular dosing are not interchangeable. Use the lowest effective dose and taper after prolonged or high-dose therapy.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses with food, preferably in the morning when once daily. Administer IV formulations at the recommended dilution and rate. Do not stop abruptly after prolonged therapy; follow the prescribed taper.
card.contraindications: Systemic fungal infection and hypersensitivity. Live vaccines are generally contraindicated during immunosuppressive doses.
card.warnings: Can cause adrenal suppression, serious infection, hyperglycemia, hypertension, fluid retention, GI bleeding, osteoporosis, psychiatric effects, cataracts, glaucoma, and growth suppression. Rapid withdrawal can cause adrenal crisis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Increased appetite, insomnia, mood changes, dyspepsia, fluid retention, elevated glucose, acne, and facial rounding with longer use.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Adrenal crisis, severe infection, GI bleeding or perforation, psychosis, pancreatitis, osteonecrosis, severe hyperglycemia, hypertension, heart failure, glaucoma, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: NSAIDs and alcohol increase GI risk. Diuretics and amphotericin increase potassium loss. Diabetes drugs may require adjustment. CYP3A4 inhibitors increase exposure. Live vaccines increase infection risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, glucose, weight, edema, electrolytes, infection, GI bleeding, mood and sleep, bone health, eye symptoms, growth in children, and adrenal suppression with long-term therapy.
card.patientTeaching: Take with food and follow the taper exactly. Report fever, black stools, severe abdominal pain, vision changes, severe mood changes, swelling, or symptoms of high glucose. Avoid sick contacts and live vaccines unless approved.
card.nutrition: Take with food. Follow guidance for sodium restriction, adequate potassium, calcium, vitamin D, and protein. Monitor carbohydrate intake if glucose rises.

card.indications: Short-term treatment of symptomatic gastroesophageal reflux that fails conventional therapy, relief of diabetic gastroparesis, and prevention or treatment of nausea and vomiting in selected settings.
card.mechanism: Blocks dopamine D2 receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone and enhances upper GI motility by increasing acetylcholine effects, accelerating gastric emptying without stimulating gastric acid secretion.
card.dosing: Regimens depend on indication and route. Oral therapy is often given 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime. Avoid treatment longer than 12 weeks except in rare cases. Reduce dose in renal impairment and older adults.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime when prescribed. Administer IV doses slowly to reduce acute dystonic reactions. Verify cumulative duration of therapy and assess for abnormal movements before each dose.
card.contraindications: GI hemorrhage, mechanical obstruction or perforation, pheochromocytoma, epilepsy, history of tardive dyskinesia or dystonic reaction to metoclopramide, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: can cause tardive dyskinesia, which may be irreversible, with risk increasing with cumulative dose and duration. Other risks include acute dystonia, parkinsonism, depression, suicidal thoughts, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, and hypertension.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Drowsiness, fatigue, restlessness, diarrhea, headache, insomnia, and mild extrapyramidal symptoms.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Tardive dyskinesia, acute dystonic reactions, neuroleptic malignant syndrome, severe depression or suicidality, seizures, hypertensive crisis, methemoglobinemia, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Antipsychotics increase extrapyramidal and NMS risk. CNS depressants increase sedation. Levodopa and dopamine agonists oppose its action. Serotonergic drugs may increase serotonin-syndrome risk.
card.monitoring: Monitor nausea or gastric-emptying response, bowel sounds, abnormal involuntary movements, rigidity, restlessness, mental status, mood, blood pressure, renal function, and treatment duration.
card.patientTeaching: Report lip smacking, tongue movements, facial grimacing, tremor, stiffness, severe restlessness, fever, or mood changes immediately. Avoid driving until effects are known and do not continue beyond the prescribed duration.
card.nutrition: Take oral doses 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime when directed. Small frequent meals may help gastroparesis. Avoid alcohol because it increases sedation.
